topics.topic: The entry-level job and internship crunch of 2026
topics.tone_reason: High-stakes, anxiety-adjacent topic for nearly every student; needs a grounded, factual tone rather than meme humor so it reads as credible and useful, not mocking.
topics.content_angle: Frame as 'what's actually hiring vs. what's frozen' rather than pure doom, using the engineering/construction/small-business hiring counterpoint.
title_options: Why Your Internship Search Feels Impossible Right Now
The 2026 Job Market, Explained in 4 Numbers
Hiring Freeze or Hiring Boom? It Depends Where You Look

slide_structure.copy: Applied to 100 internships and heard nothing back? You're not imagining it.
slide_structure.visual_direction: Bold stat-card style, dark background, large white text.

slide_structure.copy: Internship postings on Handshake dropped more than 15% between 2023 and 2025.
Applications per internship posting nearly doubled in 2025 — meaning way more competition for fewer spots.
Entry-level hiring is down 6% year-over-year, and 21% of companies say they've frozen entry-level hiring partly because of AI.
But it's not all bad: NACE still expects employers to hire 3.9% more interns this cycle than last year.
slide_structure.visual_direction: Upward trend arrow in accent color.

slide_structure.copy: Drop a comment: how many applications have you sent out this cycle?
slide_structure.visual_direction: Simple text card with comment icon.

topics.caption: The internship market in one word: brutal. Here's what the data actually says about who's hiring and who's frozen right now. Sources linked in our profile. #collegelife #internship #jobsearch #genz #careeradvice
topics.comment_prompt: How many internship applications have you sent out this cycle? Drop your number below.
topics.expected_reaction: Students tagging friends going through the same job search; comments sharing application-count horror stories.

risk_notes: Keep tone factual, avoid doom framing that could increase anxiety without the counterpoint slide.

topics.topic: Federal student loan overhaul takes effect July 1, 2026
topics.tone_reason: A policy/deadline story; needs clarity and urgency without alarmism since it affects real financial decisions.
topics.content_angle: Lead with the July 1 deadline and a 3-item 'check this before you register' checklist.
title_options: Your Student Loans Are About to Change. Here's What to Know Before July 1
3 Things to Check Before Federal Loan Rules Change This Summer
The SAVE Plan Is Ending. What That Means for You

slide_structure.copy: If you're borrowing for school this fall, a hard deadline just hit your timeline: July 1, 2026.
New federal borrowing caps start, Grad PLUS loans are ending, and Parent PLUS loans are getting limited.
Repayment plans are being consolidated into a smaller set of options going forward.
Loan limits will be prorated by enrollment status, which could leave a gap below your actual cost of attendance.
Check your loan type, your school's financial aid office, and your repayment plan before you register for fall classes.

topics.caption: Borrowing for school this fall? Federal loan rules are changing July 1 — here's the quick breakdown before you register. Always confirm details with your school's financial aid office. #studentloans #financialaid #collegelife #2026
topics.comment_prompt: Are you switching repayment plans before the deadline, or still figuring it out?
topics.expected_reaction: Borrowers tagging roommates/friends; questions about how the caps apply to their specific loan type.

claims_to_double_check: Exact dollar caps by loan type/year, which vary and should be confirmed directly with studentaid.gov before publishing exact figures
risk_notes: Add a disclaimer-style note encouraging students to confirm details with their financial aid office; avoid giving specific individualized financial advice.

topics.topic: Some college majors have zero or negative financial ROI
topics.tone_reason: Highly shareable but editorially sensitive; needs a 'know the tradeoff' framing rather than a takedown of specific majors.
topics.content_angle: Frame as 'know the tradeoff, not a takedown' — pair the ROI data with the counterpoint that mid-career economics pay can overtake some engineering subfields.
title_options: Does Your Major Actually Pay Off? The Data Might Surprise You
The College ROI Debate: What the Numbers Actually Say
Starting Salary Isn't the Whole Story for Your Major

slide_structure.copy: Is your degree actually worth what you're paying for it? Depends on the major.
The median bachelor's degree shows about $160,000 in lifetime financial ROI.
But some majors — including art, music, religion, and psychology — show zero or negative net financial value by this analysis.
The NY Fed estimates roughly a 12.5% return on a degree overall, though the college-vs-high-school earnings gap has been stagnating.
Plot twist: some 'lower-paying' majors like economics catch up — starting near $60K but reaching ~$130K mid-career.
slide_structure.source_note: src_018 Boston Globe / src_010 Extern (background data)

slide_structure.copy: This isn't about which major is 'better' — it's about knowing the tradeoff you're making. What's your major, and does this track with your experience?

topics.caption: The college ROI debate, with actual numbers attached. Not a ranking of which major is 'better' — just the tradeoffs worth knowing. #collegemajor #roi #studentdebt #careeradvice
topics.comment_prompt: What's your major, and does this data match what you're seeing in your job search?
topics.expected_reaction: Defensive comments from students in lower-ROI majors; comparison comments between majors.

claims_to_double_check: FREOPP's ROI methodology is not independently audited here; present as one analysis, not definitive fact
risk_notes: Must avoid mocking or shaming specific majors or the students who study them; keep framing analytical, not judgmental.
do_not_say: X major is a waste of money

topics.topic: Gen Z's shift to 'poly-employment' and side hustles
topics.tone_reason: Validating, lifestyle-trend story rather than urgent news.
topics.content_angle: Position as 'this is the new normal, not a red flag' to validate students already working multiple jobs.
title_options: Why So Many of Your Classmates Have 3 Jobs Right Now
Poly-Employment: The Gen Z Work Trend Nobody Planned For
One Job Isn't the Default Anymore — Here's the Data

slide_structure.copy: If it feels like everyone you know has more than one job, that's because they do.
57% of Gen Z workers have a side hustle, compared to just 21% of Boomers.
Gen Z makes up 55% of all 'poly-employed' workers juggling multiple part-time roles.

topics.tone_reason: Classic ranking format; informational rather than provocative.
topics.content_angle: Pair the starting-salary ranking with the economics 'long game' twist to avoid being a flat, predictable Top 10.
title_options: The Highest-Paying College Majors in 2026
Which Major Pays the Most Right Out of College?
Starting Salary by Major: 2026 Breakdown

slide_structure.copy: Curious which majors actually pay the most starting out? Here's the 2026 breakdown.
Petroleum Engineering leads at roughly $83K starting; Computer Engineering follows near $80K.
But Economics grads starting around $60K can reach ~$130K mid-career — sometimes overtaking engineering subfields long-term.

topics.topic: Students boo AI at commencement but still use it to cheat
topics.tone_reason: Polarizing topic; needs a 'where do you draw the line' framing, never instructional about cheating.
topics.content_angle: Frame around the contradiction/cognitive dissonance, not as a how-to; ask 'where do you draw the line?' rather than mocking either side.
title_options: Students Are Booing AI at Graduation. They're Still Using It Daily
The AI Contradiction No One on Campus Wants to Admit
57% of Students Use AI Weekly. So Why the Backlash?

slide_structure.copy: Students booed AI at commencement this year. Most of them used it to write a paper last week.
57% of college students use AI weekly for coursework; daily users self-report cheating at 26%, vs. 7% for monthly users.

topics.topic: US and Canada both tighten the door on international students
topics.tone_reason: Politically sensitive policy topic; stay factual and neutral.
topics.content_angle: Side-by-side 'what changed in the US vs. Canada' comparison, staying neutral on the underlying policy debate.
title_options: US and Canada Are Both Cutting Back on International Students
What's Changing for International Students in 2026
Studying Abroad in North America Just Got Harder

slide_structure.copy: Whether you're applying to the US or Canada, the door just got a little narrower.
Canada capped 2026 study permit spaces at 309,670.
New US student visa issuance dropped 35.6% last summer amid tighter screening.

topics.topic: College student mental health is actually improving (but loneliness persists)
topics.tone_reason: Sensitive mental-health topic; must avoid dismissing students still struggling.
topics.content_angle: Lead with the surprising 'actually getting better' framing but immediately acknowledge loneliness and substance use are still real.
title_options: Mental Health on Campus Is Actually Improving, Data Shows

slide_structure.copy: National data shows depression and anxiety rates among college students have declined for a third straight year — though loneliness still affects over half of students.

topics.topic: Record freshman classes are causing a campus housing crunch
topics.content_angle: Use named examples (Pitt, ACU) as illustrations of a 'is this happening at your school too?' prompt rather than claiming a definitive national crisis.
title_options: Record Freshman Classes Are Straining Dorm Space

slide_structure.copy: Pitt is building a new dorm after record applications strained freshman housing; Abilene Christian converted buildings to all-triple rooms.
slide_structure.source_note: src_011 Pittsburgh Post-Gazette / src_012 The Optimist

topics.topic: TikTok still dominates how college-age students spend their day
topics.tone_reason: Low-confidence data with a population mismatch; keep minimal and caveat clearly if produced at all.
topics.content_angle: If produced, stick to the verified Pew data and clearly note it covers teens, not making any unverified claims specifically about college students.
title_options: How Much Time Do Teens Spend on TikTok? (Data, Not College-Specific)

slide_structure.copy: Teens 13-17 spend about 1h35m a day on TikTok, per Pew. Note: this is teen data, not college-student-specific.
